contact.when_to_contact: Best reached on cell weekdays after 4:30 PM and anytime on weekends; please avoid calling after 9:30 PM Pacific unless it is urgent. Email is checked frequently throughout the day, even while at work. If unavailable, you may also contact my spouse, Emily, at the home number listed in the insured information section.

loss_information.describe_location_of_loss_if_not_at_spec: Two-story wood-frame single-family residence located on Bayview Terrace in Tacoma’s North End neighborhood, approximately three blocks uphill from Ruston Way Waterfront and facing west toward Commencement Bay. The loss primarily affected the finished basement level and portions of the first floor at the rear (bay-facing) side of the home.
loss_information.description_of_loss_damage:
On the evening of 2025-08-18, a severe windstorm moved through the Tacoma, WA area with sustained winds reportedly between 40–50 mph and stronger gusts. At approximately 7:45 PM Pacific, a strong gust caused a large section of an older Douglas fir tree located in our backyard (on our property, near the rear fence line) to snap and fall directly onto the back portion of our house.

The falling tree limb and trunk segment struck the rear roofline above the kitchen and dining room area, causing immediate structural and water damage. The impact ripped away a section of asphalt shingles and underlying plywood sheathing, created a visible hole roughly 4 ft by 6 ft in the roof, and displaced several rafters. The force of the impact also cracked part of the exterior siding above the sliding glass door that opens from the dining room onto the rear deck.

Because the storm was still active and it was raining steadily at the time, water began entering through the damaged roof area almost immediately. Within about 15–20 minutes, we noticed water staining and active dripping from the kitchen ceiling near a recessed light fixture and along one of the ceiling seams. The water then migrated down into the wall cavities and eventually pooled on the main floor and seeped down into the finished basement family room directly below the kitchen.

By about 8:15 PM, we observed water coming through the basement ceiling drywall in several spots, particularly near a ceiling-mounted light and along a taped joint. The basement carpeting became progressively soaked over an area of approximately 12 ft by 16 ft. We placed towels, buckets, and plastic sheeting where we could, but the volume and spread of the water were more than we could manage ourselves. Out of concern for electrical hazards and potential ceiling collapse, we shut off power to the affected circuits at the breaker panel.

Major categories of damage observed so far include:

1) ROOF AND STRUCTURAL DAMAGE:
- Large hole in rear section of roof above kitchen/dining area (estimated 4 ft x 6 ft), with missing shingles, torn underlayment, and broken sheathing.
- Several cracked and displaced rafters in the affected bay; visible sagging of the roofline from the backyard.
- Damaged gutters and fascia at the rear eave where the tree impacted; about 12–15 ft of guttering appears bent or torn away.
- Splintered sections of the fallen tree remain on the roof and in the backyard; we have not attempted full removal for safety reasons.

2) INTERIOR WATER DAMAGE – MAIN FLOOR:
- Water stains and bubbling/peeling paint on the kitchen ceiling and upper walls adjacent to the exterior rear wall.
- Saturated insulation likely present in the ceiling cavities above the kitchen, though this has not yet been opened up.
- Several seams in the drywall ceiling are visibly cracked or swollen; one tape joint is starting to separate.
- The wood trim around the rear sliding glass door is damp to the touch, and the door is now slightly harder to open and close, suggesting swelling or minor warping.
- Some water tracked further into the dining room hardwood flooring; a few boards are beginning to cup and discolor.

3) INTERIOR WATER DAMAGE – FINISHED BASEMENT:
- Ceiling drywall directly below the kitchen shows multiple wet spots and sagging areas. One section (roughly 2 ft by 3 ft) is noticeably bowed and may need to be removed to prevent collapse and mold growth.
- Recessed basement light fixtures in the affected area had water dripping from the trim rings before we shut off the power.
- Wall-to-wall carpet in the basement family room is saturated in a roughly 12 ft by 16 ft area, with water having seeped into the carpet pad beneath. There is a musty odor already beginning to develop.
- The lower 6–8 inches of the interior drywall on at least two basement walls feel damp and cool, which may indicate that water has wicked up from the floor. Baseboards in these areas are also damp.

4) PERSONAL PROPERTY DAMAGE:
- A medium-sized fabric sectional sofa located in the basement family room under the affected ceiling area was directly impacted by dripping water. The back cushions and one armrest are heavily soaked.
- A 5 ft by 8 ft area rug and several throw pillows on the basement floor are waterlogged.
- A wooden IKEA-style media console on the basement wall beneath one of the ceiling leaks has visible water staining on the top surface and slight swelling along its back edge.
- An older 55" flat-screen TV on that console was splashed by water droplets. We immediately unplugged it and have not attempted to power it back on; potential internal damage is unknown at this time.

5) SAFETY AND MITIGATION STEPS TAKEN:
- Immediately after the incident, I (Daniel Whitaker) shut off power to the kitchen and basement family room circuits at the main breaker panel to reduce the risk of electrical shock and fire.
- We moved smaller items (books, toys, electronics) away from the affected basement area and propped up some furniture on plastic bins to keep it off the wet carpet.
- We placed buckets and large mixing bowls under the most active drips in both the kitchen and basement and used old towels and blankets to soak up standing water on the floors.
- We contacted a local emergency roofing contractor, who arrived later that night around 10:15 PM. They installed a temporary tarp over the damaged section of the roof to prevent further water intrusion. They advised that a full roof repair and possible partial structural rebuild will be needed.
- We also scheduled a water mitigation company (RainCity Restoration Services) for the following morning to begin professional water extraction, dehumidification, and removal of any unsalvageable materials (wet drywall, soaked carpet pad, etc.). They have taken initial moisture readings, documented affected areas, and started running fans and dehumidifiers.

No one in our household was physically injured during the incident. We were all at home at the time, and once we heard the loud cracking and impact, we moved away from the back of the house and ensured our two children (ages 7 and 10) stayed upstairs in the front bedrooms until we confirmed it was safe. We have not noticed any obvious foundation damage, but we are not qualified to assess structural integrity beyond what is visibly apparent. We are particularly concerned about the long-term effects of hidden moisture in the wall and ceiling cavities, potential mold growth, and whether the roof structure needs partial reconstruction.

We have taken numerous photos and short videos of the damage, including exterior shots of the fallen tree on the roof, interior ceiling and wall damage, and the affected basement carpeting and furnishings. We are prepared to provide these to the adjuster upon request. We have not yet obtained any formal written repair estimates, but based on preliminary conversations with the emergency roofer and restoration company, we understand that the combined cost of roof repair, interior demolition and rebuild, and potential replacement of damaged personal property may be substantial.

To the best of my knowledge, there was no pre-existing roof leak in this area. The roof is approximately 13 years old (architectural asphalt shingles installed in 2012), and while we had been planning to budget for a replacement in the next several years, we had not experienced active leaking prior to this storm event. The tree that failed appeared healthy from the outside, though the broken cross-section now shows some internal decay that we were unaware of. We will also be seeking guidance on whether the cost to remove the remaining unstable portions of the tree is covered under our policy.
loss_information.reported_to: Melissa J. Carver, HarborPoint Insurance Services, LLC
